records.title: A linear time algorithm for finding tree-decompositions of small treewidth
records.citation: Hans L. Bodlaender (1993). A linear time algorithm for finding tree-decompositions of small treewidth. https://doi.org/10.1145/167088.167161
records.summary: In this paper, we give, for constant k, a linear time algorithm, that given a graph G = (V, E), determines whether the treewidth of G is at most k, and if so, finds a treedecomposition of G with treewidth at most k. A consequence is that every minor-closed class of graphs that does not contain all planar graphs has a l...
claims: In this paper, we give, for constant k, a linear time algorithm, that given a graph G = (V, E), determines whether the treewidth of G is at most k, and if so, finds a treedecomposition of G with treewidth at most k. A consequence is that every minor-closed class of graphs that does not contain all planar graphs has a l...

records.title: A Knowledge Compilation Map
records.citation: A. Darwiche, P. Marquis (2002). A Knowledge Compilation Map. Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research. https://doi.org/10.1613/jair.989
records.summary: We propose a perspective on knowledge compilation which calls for analyzing different compilation approaches according to two key dimensions: the succinctness of the target compilation language, and the class of queries and transformations that the language supports in polytime. We then provide a knowledge compilation...
assumptions: Compilation is offline amortized over many online queries.
Representation language tradeoffs can be formalized by tractable operations.
Compilation target language properties determine query tractability and transformation costs.
contributions: Systematic map of NNF-family target languages and tractable operations.
Taxonomy and complexity map of knowledge compilation languages.
claims: Different compilation targets provide different succinctness-vs-tractability tradeoffs.
Language choice governs feasible tractable operations post-compilation.
conclusions: Choose the most succinct language that still supports required polytime operations.
Compilation is powerful but representation selection is critical.
limitations: Focuses on propositional settings and known language families.
Compilation blow-up can dominate for unfavorable structure.
future_work: Extend map to additional target languages and operation sets.
Refined language design and practical compiler strategies.
key_equations: target language choice is governed by succinctness and polytime query/transformation support

evidence_atoms.content: Compilation map links succinctness to polytime query/transformation support.

records.title: DPLL with a Trace: From SAT to Knowledge Compilation
records.citation: Huang, J., and Darwiche, A. (2005). DPLL with a Trace: From SAT to Knowledge Compilation. IJCAI 2005. https://www.ijcai.org/Proceedings/05/Papers/0876.pdf
records.summary: The paper connects exhaustive DPLL traces to knowledge-compilation targets and shows how restrictions map traces into d-DNNF, FBDD, and OBDD families.
assumptions: Exhaustive DPLL traces are recorded as DAGs.
Trace language membership determines tractable query classes.
Structured traces of search can enable reusable inference computations.
contributions: Unified SAT-search to compilation perspective.
Language-membership view for understanding search algorithm capabilities.
Trace-based extension to DPLL-style solving.
claims: Compiled traces can land in d-DNNF/FBDD/OBDD depending on search constraints.
Trace capture improves reusability of search-derived reasoning artifacts.
conclusions: Compilation and SAT search can be studied under one framework with clear tractability tradeoffs.
Search traces are useful intermediates for exact reasoning tasks.
limitations: Trace classes from common solvers may occupy strict subsets (e.g., restricted d-DNNF fragments).
Benefit depends on structural reuse patterns.
future_work: Design compilers/solvers that move beyond restricted trace subsets.
Richer trace formalisms and integrations with counting.

evidence_atoms.content: DPLL traces correspond to compiled artifacts in d-DNNF/FBDD/OBDD families.

records.title: On probabilistic inference by weighted model counting
records.citation: Chavira, M., and Darwiche, A. (2008). On probabilistic inference by weighted model counting. Artificial Intelligence 172(6-7), 772-799. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.artint.2007.11.002
records.summary: This work formalizes exact probabilistic inference as weighted model counting over CNF encodings and studies how encoding, counting backend, and local structure influence performance.
assumptions: Probabilistic models can be encoded as weighted CNF.
Exact inference can exploit determinism and local structure after encoding.
Probabilistic inference can be reduced to weighted counting over compiled forms.
contributions: Comparative analysis of WMC-based exact inference design choices.
Empirical evidence that WMC/compilation can expand tractable exact inference regimes.
Formal link between probabilistic inference and model counting.
claims: Encoding and compilation choices substantially affect exact inference scalability.
WMC provides a unifying lens for exact probabilistic inference complexity.
conclusions: WMC-based formulations are effective for exact inference when structure is exploitable.
Compilation+counting pipelines are principled but structure-sensitive.
limitations: Performance remains sensitive to model structure and encoding quality.
Complexity remains high for large/high-treewidth instances.
future_work: Further improve encodings and hybrid exact/approximate strategies.
More efficient counting/compilation methods.
key_equations: Pr(evidence) is computed from weighted counts over CNF models consistent with evidence.

evidence_atoms.content: Probabilistic inference is reduced to weighted model counting on CNF.
Encode Bayesian network and evidence into weighted CNF then count weighted satisfying assignments.

records.title: DeepProbLog: Neural Probabilistic Logic Programming
records.citation: Robin Manhaeve, Sebastijan Dumančić, Angelika Kimmig, Thomas Demeester (2018). DeepProbLog: Neural Probabilistic Logic Programming. ORCA Online Research @Cardiff (Cardiff University). https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.1805.10872
records.summary: We introduce DeepProbLog, a probabilistic logic programming language that incorporates deep learning by means of neural predicates. We show how existing inference and learning techniques can be adapted for the new language. Our experiments demonstrate that DeepProbLog supports both symbolic and subsymbolic representati...
assumptions: ProbLog semantics with neural-parameterized probabilistic facts.
Gradient flow from logic loss to neural modules is available.
contributions: DeepProbLog unifies ProbLog inference with trainable neural predicates.
claims: Supports symbolic+subsymbolic inference, program induction, and end-to-end training.
conclusions: Neural probabilistic logic programming supports compositional neuro-symbolic tasks.
limitations: Inference speed depends on grounding and compilation complexity.
future_work: Broader scalable approximate inference and larger domains.
key_equations: Neural predicates supply probabilistic facts whose probabilities are outputs of neural networks.

evidence_atoms.content: DeepProbLog introduces neural predicates and adapts inference/learning accordingly.
Experiments optimize cross-entropy and backpropagate through neural predicates.

records.title: NeurASP: Embracing Neural Networks into Answer Set Programming
records.citation: Zhun Yang, Adam Ishay, Joohyung Lee (2020). NeurASP: Embracing Neural Networks into Answer Set Programming. https://doi.org/10.24963/ijcai.2020/243
records.summary: We present NeurASP, a simple extension of answer set programs by embracing neural networks. By treating the neural network output as the probability distribution over atomic facts in answer set programs, NeurASP provides a simple and effective way to integrate sub-symbolic and symbolic computation. We demonstrate how N...
assumptions: Neural outputs are calibrated as probabilities over neural atoms.
Stable-model semantics governs symbolic part.
Neural predictions can be coupled with symbolic constraints through ASP semantics.
contributions: NeurASP framework coupling neural predicates with ASP rules and stable-model reasoning.
Neurosymbolic training framework linking neural outputs and ASP reasoning.
claims: Logical constraints can improve training and inference consistency compared with pure neural baselines.
Symbolic constraints improve consistency versus unconstrained neural models.
conclusions: NeurASP integrates symbolic constraints and neural perception in end-to-end learning.
NeurASP is an effective basis for exact neurosymbolic reasoning tasks.
limitations: Inference can be costly due to stable-model enumeration on large/high-count instances.
Inference can become expensive as program structure grows.
future_work: Scale exact inference via compilation and improved backend routing.
Scalability and inference acceleration.
key_equations: P_{Pi(theta)}(I) = product over neural atoms of predicted probabilities for assignments in I

evidence_atoms.content: Stable models are defined over transformed program with neural atoms.
Public implementation and experiments are provided in NeurASP repository.

records.title: Treewidth-Aware Cycle Breaking for Algebraic Answer Set Counting
records.citation: Thomas Eiter, Markus Hecher, Rafael Kiesel (2021). Treewidth-Aware Cycle Breaking for Algebraic Answer Set Counting. Proceedings of KR 2021. https://proceedings.kr.org/2021/26/kr2021-0026-eiter-et-al.pdf
records.summary: Introduces TP-unfolding to break positive-dependency cycles in normal ASP while preserving answer sets bijectively, enabling stronger treewidth-aware reductions to algebraic model counting pipelines.
assumptions: Programs are normal ASP programs with stable-model semantics.
Cycle breaking should preserve model bijectivity for counting-based objectives.
Compilation cost is sensitive to structural width after transformation.
Cycle handling under bounded structural width can preserve semantics while reducing complexity.
contributions: Defines TP-unfolding as a semantics-preserving cycle-breaking mechanism for AASC.
Provides treewidth-aware correctness/runtime analysis for transformed encodings.
Empirically compares cycle-breaking variants under counting workloads.
Treewidth-aware cycle-breaking methods for answer set counting.
claims: Treewidth-aware cycle breaking can improve downstream algebraic counting efficiency compared with prior bijective reductions.
Model-preserving transformations are critical for probabilistic and weighted ASP inference.
Structure-guided transformations improve tractability in counting settings.
conclusions: TP-unfolding broadens practical AASC applicability by reducing structural burden before compilation.
No single cycle-breaking strategy dominates for every instance family.
Cycle-breaking strategy should be conditioned on structural regime.
limitations: Performance gains vary by graph structure and SCC composition.
Transformation overhead can offset improvements on small/easy instances.
Effectiveness depends on program topology and decomposition quality.
future_work: Adaptive selection among cycle-breaking operators based on structural diagnostics.
Stronger integration with learned backend selectors for mixed workloads.
Broader transformation libraries and integrated structure-aware compilers.
notation_seeds: SCC for strongly connected component size

evidence_atoms.content: Treewidth-aware cycle breaking can improve downstream algebraic counting efficiency compared with prior bijective reductions.
treewidth(G) = min_{(T,chi)} max_{t in V(T)} |chi(t)| - 1

records.title: Treewidth-aware reductions of normal ASP to SAT - Is normal ASP harder than SAT after all?
records.citation: Markus Hecher, Roland Kiesel, Michael Morak, Johannes K. Fichte, Stefan Szeider (2021). Treewidth-aware reductions of normal ASP to SAT - Is normal ASP harder than SAT after all? https://doi.org/10.1016/j.artint.2021.103651
records.summary: Presents a treewidth-aware reduction from normal ASP to SAT and proves ETH-tight lower bounds showing unavoidable treewidth overhead, clarifying structural hardness separation from SAT.
assumptions: Exponential Time Hypothesis (ETH) is assumed for lower-bound claims.
Input programs are normal/HCF ASP instances with dependency-graph structure.
Treewidth-preserving reductions can transfer tractability from SAT to ASP instances.
contributions: Defines reduction that keeps explicit control over treewidth growth from ASP to SAT.
Proves ETH-tightness of treewidth overhead for the reduction family.
Provides empirical comparison against existing translations using decomposition heuristics.
Formal treewidth-aware reductions and complexity characterization.
claims: Normal ASP is structurally harder than SAT under treewidth-sensitive complexity assumptions.
Sub-quadratic elimination of treewidth overhead in generic reductions is unlikely under ETH.
Normal ASP can be made closer to SAT complexity under structure-aware reductions.
conclusions: Treewidth-aware translation must be paired with ASP-specific optimizations rather than generic SAT reduction alone.
Complexity-theoretic bounds justify explicit routing decisions between ASP-native and compiled backends.
Reduction design materially impacts practical exact inference runtime.
limitations: Lower bounds are conditional on ETH.
Empirical section depends on decomposition heuristic quality and benchmark mix.
Advantages depend on decomposition quality and reduction overhead.
future_work: Explore restricted program classes with improved practical constants.
Hybrid reductions that exploit additional structural parameters beyond treewidth.
Stronger reduction templates for broader ASP fragments.
key_equations: runtime lower bound under ETH: no 2^{o(k*log(k))} * poly(n) algorithm for normal ASP by treewidth k
reduction overhead implies SAT treewidth tracks ASP structure up to provable asymptotic inflation
notation_seeds: SCC for strongly connected component size

evidence_atoms.content: Normal ASP is structurally harder than SAT under treewidth-sensitive complexity assumptions.

records.title: Interpretable Neural-Symbolic Concept Reasoning
records.citation: Pietro Barbiero, Gabriele Ciravegna, Francesco Giannini, Mateo Espinosa Zarlenga (2023). Interpretable Neural-Symbolic Concept Reasoning. arXiv (Cornell University). https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2304.14068

assumptions: Scalable neural-probabilistic ASP requires tractable approximation/truncation over probabilistic choices.
Careful inference design can scale NP-ASP to larger tasks while retaining useful accuracy.
contributions: SLASH/SAME-style scalable neural-probabilistic ASP pipeline with extensive experiments.
Scalability strategies for NP-ASP inference and training.
claims: Demonstrates efficiency/scalability on VQA and MNIST-addition style tasks.
Runtime improvements on neurosymbolic ASP benchmarks.
conclusions: Scalable variants make NP-ASP practical on larger datasets.
Scaling techniques broaden practical deployment of NP-ASP.
limitations: Approximation/truncation choices can influence exactness and calibration.
Efficiency-accuracy tradeoffs depend on approximation/configuration choices.
future_work: Improve exactness-efficiency tradeoffs and broader benchmarks.
Better exactness-runtime balancing for larger structures.

evidence_atoms.content: Experiments use top-99% mass and multiple seeds in ablations.

records.title: Characterizing Structural Hardness of Logic Programs: What makes Cycles and Reachability Hard for Treewidth?
records.citation: Markus Hecher, Georg I. Roos, Roland Kiesel, Stefan Szeider (2023). Characterizing Structural Hardness of Logic Programs: What Makes Cycles and Reachability Hard for Treewidth? https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.07472
records.summary: Characterizes how cycle size and reachability structure drive treewidth hardness in normal ASP, including SAT-to-ASP reductions that exploit structural power and establish near-optimal hardness dependencies.
assumptions: ETH-based parameterized complexity framework.
Structural hardness is evaluated on dependency-graph treewidth and SCC size.
Not all hard structures are eliminated by low-width decompositions.
contributions: Provides SAT-to-normal-ASP reduction reducing treewidth to O(k/log(k)) in the constructed regime.
Separates role of reachability and cycle size in hardness characterizations.
Supplies theorem-level bounds linking SCC structure to algorithmic limits.
Structural hardness analysis for cycles/reachability in logic programs.
claims: Cycles/reachability are principal structural bottlenecks for treewidth-parameterized ASP solving.
Known hardness dependence is close to optimal under ETH.
Specific motifs induce persistent hardness even with treewidth-aware treatment.
conclusions: Structural analysis should inform rewrite targets beyond raw clause count minimization.
Cycle-aware transformations are necessary to unlock robust treewidth improvements.
Routers should account for hardness motifs beyond raw treewidth.
limitations: Theoretical emphasis; practical solver integration is limited.
Results are anchored in worst-case analysis.
Findings are structure-family specific and require careful transfer to new domains.
future_work: Translate hardness characterizations into actionable preprocessing heuristics.
Extend analysis to richer ASP fragments and probabilistic variants.
Motif-aware transformations and richer hardness predictors.
key_equations: runtime scale for normal ASP under treewidth: 2^{O(k*log(k))} * poly(n)
cycle-size bound in constructed dependency graphs: |SCC| <= 2^{O(k*log(k))}
notation_seeds: SCC for strongly connected component size

evidence_atoms.locator: knowledge/text/arxiv_2301_07472.txt: abstract and contributions block lines 1-80
evidence_atoms.content: Cycles/reachability are principal structural bottlenecks for treewidth-parameterized ASP solving.
runtime scale for normal ASP under treewidth: 2^{O(k*log(k))} * poly(n)
ETH-based parameterized complexity framework.
Construct SAT-to-ASP structural reduction

records.title: The Impact of Structure in Answer Set Counting: Fighting Cycles and its Limits
records.citation: Markus Hecher, Roland Kiesel, Georg I. Roos, Stefan Szeider (2023). The Impact of Structure in Answer Set Counting: Fighting Cycles and its Limits. KR 2023. https://proceedings.kr.org/2023/34/kr2023-0034-hecher-et-al.pdf
records.summary: Studies answer-set counting through cycle-size-sensitive transformations, proving runtime bounds and limits for small/medium/large cycles, and connecting ASP counting to #SAT/PMC techniques.
assumptions: Normal ASP programs with explicit cycle-breaking transformations.
ETH-based lower bounds for counting complexity comparisons.
Cycle structure critically influences counting complexity and algorithmic effectiveness.
contributions: Combines FVS-based and prior cycle-breaking ideas into tighter counting reductions.
Derives theorem-level runtime bounds for normal answer-set counting.
Analyzes limits of cycle-fighting strategies across structural regimes.
Empirical and theoretical analysis of structure-sensitive answer set counting.
claims: Cycle-aware preprocessing materially changes tractability in #ASP.
Different cycle-size regimes require distinct counting strategies.
Cycle patterns can dominate practical exact counting runtime.
conclusions: Structure-sensitive counting pipelines should pick transformations based on observed cycle regime.
Projected/model counting tools are useful for large-cycle fallback handling.
Structure-aware decision features are necessary for robust routing.
limitations: Gap remains between upper and lower bounds in medium-cycle regimes.
Practical gains depend on accurate structural diagnostics.
Transferability depends on benchmark structure diversity.
future_work: Close remaining complexity gap for medium cycles.
Automate regime detection and strategy selection in solvers.
Adaptive cycle-aware preprocessing and hybrid solving strategies.
key_equations: runtime bounds depend on treewidth k and feedback-vertex-set size |f_S| through O(k*log(|f_S|)) style terms
notation_seeds: SCC for strongly connected component size

evidence_atoms.content: Cycle-aware preprocessing materially changes tractability in #ASP.
#ASP(Pi) = #SAT(Clark(Pi_FVS)) under correctness-preserving cycle breaking
Normal ASP programs with explicit cycle-breaking transformations.
Apply Pi_FVS cycle-breaking transformation

records.title: aspmc: New frontiers of algebraic answer set counting
records.citation: Eiter, T., Hecher, M., and Kiesel, R. (2024). aspmc: New frontiers of algebraic answer set counting. Artificial Intelligence 330. https://repositum.tuwien.at/bitstream/20.500.12708/209319/1/Eiter-2024-Artificial%20Intelligence-vor.pdf
records.summary: The paper extends algebraic answer set counting with treewidth-aware completion and cycle-breaking strategies, and evaluates resulting CNF/compilation efficiency under multiple encodings.
assumptions: Programs are normal ASP programs after preprocessing.
Compilation performance depends strongly on CNF size and structural width.
contributions: Improved Clark-completion variants with stronger width guarantees.
Treewidth-aware cycle breaking and backend choices for algebraic counting.
Extensive empirical analysis of CNF size, width, and solver/runtime effects.
claims: Incidence-treewidth-aware transformations can reduce compilation burden compared with naive encodings.
Encoding choices materially change downstream counting and learning performance.
conclusions: Treewidth-sensitive transformations are practical and improve algebraic ASP counting workflows.
limitations: Benefits are instance- and structure-dependent; no single transformation dominates all workloads.
future_work: Improve compiler integration and reduce worst-case exponential width dependence.

evidence_atoms.content: treewidth uses max bag size minus one over all decompositions
incidence treewidth gives tighter or equal bounds than primal treewidth in their setting

records.title: Treewidth-Aware Neurosymbolic ASP via Algebraic Model Counting (user-provided manuscript)
records.citation: User Upload (2026). Treewidth-Aware Neurosymbolic ASP via Algebraic Model Counting. Local reference resource: workspace/resources/Treewidth_Aware_Neurosymbolic_ASPvia_Algebraic_Model_Counting__2_.pdf.
records.summary: Defines a dual-backend neurosymbolic ASP framing (enumeration and compilation), motivating treewidth-aware routing and benchmark families relevant to AutoTW-ASP.
assumptions: Stable-model semantics must be preserved by any optimization or rewrite.
contributions: Frames exact neurosymbolic ASP training with backend crossover analysis.
claims: Structure-aware backend routing can reduce runtime while preserving correctness.
conclusions: Automated rewrite and routing are a natural extension over manual encoding redesign.
limitations: Uploaded manuscript metadata is anonymized for formal bibliography attribution.
future_work: Automated semantics-preserving rewrites and calibrated backend routers.

evidence_atoms.content: Backend selection should depend on structural width and reuse behavior.

future_work: Scalability and richer symbolic constraints.
